name: Okuninushi
description: Okuninushi, also known as Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, is a prominent deity in Japanese mythology, primarily associated with nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protection. He is considered a benevolent and compassionate figure, often depicted as a wise ruler who brought prosperity and stability to the land. Unlike some of the more overtly powerful and martial deities, Okuninushi's strength lies in his diplomatic skills and his ability to forge alliances. He is a central figure in the Izumo cycle of myths, where he undergoes numerous trials and tribulations, ultimately proving his worthiness to rule. His story highlights themes of perseverance, compassion, and the importance of establishing harmonious relationships between humans and the divine. He is greatly revered and enshrined in many Shinto shrines throughout Japan, most notably at Izumo Taisha.
subtitle: Great Lord of the Land

keyMyths.title: The Hare of Inaba
keyMyths.description: Okuninushi, then known as Ōnamuchi, was tasked with carrying the bags of his brothers on their journey to woo Princess Yakami of Inaba. He lagged behind, and upon arriving, he found a skinned hare, crying in pain. He instructed the hare to bathe in fresh water and roll in cattails. The hare followed his instructions and felt immediate relief. The hare then revealed himself to be a deity and prophesied that Ōnamuchi would win the princess' hand. This myth highlights Okuninushi's compassion and his connection to the natural world.

keyMyths.title: The Trials of Susano-o
keyMyths.description: After facing numerous attempts on his life by his jealous brothers, Okuninushi sought refuge in the underworld, where he met Susano-o. Susano-o subjected him to a series of trials, including sleeping in a room full of snakes and centipedes, and shooting an arrow into a vast field. With the help of Susano-o's daughter, Suseri-hime, Okuninushi overcame these trials and escaped with Susano-o's sword, bow, and koto. This myth demonstrates Okuninushi's resourcefulness and his ability to learn from powerful figures.

keyMyths.title: The Transfer of the Land
keyMyths.description: After establishing his rule over Izumo, Okuninushi was approached by deities from Takamagahara (the heavenly realm), who demanded that he relinquish control of the land to the descendants of Amaterasu. Initially resistant, Okuninushi eventually agreed, provided that a grand shrine be built in his honor at Izumo. This act of selflessness and willingness to compromise solidified his reputation as a wise and benevolent ruler, ensuring his continued veneration even after stepping down from power. This myth also highlights the complex relationship between the earthly and heavenly realms in Japanese mythology.
